frameworks.name: robust averaging
frameworks.one_line: decision weights downweight extreme evidence relative to inlying items

frameworks.name: active sensing / saccadic information sampling
frameworks.one_line: gaze policy selects which spatial items to fixate during evidence accumulation

frameworks.name: salience-driven gaze in visual search and scene viewing
frameworks.one_line: literature predicts preferential fixation of deviant, diagnostic items

core_question: How do humans direct their eyes to sample evidence when a perceptual decision must integrate information across multiple spatial locations?
key_assumptions: averaging task requires combining evidence from many spatial elements
fixation choice reflects an internal sampling policy
diagnosticity of an element is its distance from the reference value
manipulations: spatial array of numbers with varying distance from a reference value
judgment of whether the mean exceeds or falls below the reference
key_findings: participants preferentially fixated inlying (less diagnostic) elements
this inlier-sampling bias predicted decision weighting
inlying items received greater weight in the final choice (robust averaging)
interpretations: humans adopt a robust sampling policy that downweights deviant evidence in perceptual decision-making
this contrasts with deviance-seeking gaze observed in search and natural-scene viewing
